name: better-git-vscode
description: Fast keyboard-driven git diff review — jump between changes and changed files, stage-and-advance, revert, all without the mouse.

commands.title: Stage current file and advance to next unstaged change
Stage current file and advance to previous unstaged change
Stage current file and advance in last-navigated direction
Smart forward (next change in a diff, else navigate forward)
Smart back (previous change in a diff, else navigate back)
Open & reveal current file in Explorer (works from staged diffs)
Open changes (diff) at current cursor/scroll position
Reopen the diff of the last file staged via Better Git
Collapse all worktree / repository sections in Source Control

better-git-vscode.treeView.description: Check this If you use Tree view (as opposed to List view)

better-git-vscode.shouldOpenScmView.description: Controls whether the source control side panel should open when going to next/previous change

better-git-vscode.revealStagedInSourceCo.description: Experimental: also select/highlight staged files in the Source Control view while navigating (works around a VS Code limitation where staged diffs can't be auto-revealed). May cause a brief flash of the file, and for partially-staged files highlights the unstaged copy. Requires scm.autoReveal (on by default).

better-git-vscode.currentFileBadge.description: The badge shown on the file you're currently reviewing, in the Source Control panel (and Explorer/tabs). Max 2 characters — an emoji renders in colour and is the most visible option (the Source Control panel ignores decoration colours). Default is double fire 🔥🔥. Loud choices: 🔥 🔴 🚨 🟥 ❗ ⭐ 👉. Tip: two emoji (e.g. 🔥🔥) is allowed and renders a bit wider. Set to empty to disable the badge.

better-git-vscode.dvorakMode.description: Keep navigation on the same PHYSICAL '>' and '<' keys under a Dvorak layout: next/previous change become alt+v / alt+w (and stage-and-advance shift+alt+v / shift+alt+w) — the characters a Dvorak keyboard types on the keys where QWERTY has '.' and ','. Same commands and behaviour as the QWERTY alt+. / alt+, defaults, just remapped characters. Your own keybindings.json entries always take precedence.

better-git-vscode.showLastStagedInStatus.description: Show a status bar item (bottom-left) with the last file you staged through this extension (stage-and-advance or the editor-title + button). Useful when stage-and-advance jumps so fast you don't notice what got staged — click the item to reopen that file's staged diff and unstage it if it was a mistake. Turn off to hide the indicator.

better-git-vscode.collapseWorktreesOnSta.description: Off by default; turn on to enable. When you have multiple git worktrees / repositories open in one window, VS Code's Source Control panel shows each as its own section and renders them all EXPANDED on every window open / reload — noisy with several worktrees. With this ON, the extension collapses the OTHER worktree/repository sections shortly after the window loads, but keeps your PRIMARY / main repository (the one at the top — your main working copy) EXPANDED, since that's the one you're working in. It only acts when there are 2+ repositories, and it briefly focuses the Source Control view to do so (a workaround because VS Code has no API to persist/default the section collapse state, nor to collapse/expand a single repository). To re-expand the primary it opens one of that repo's changed files in a preview tab, which relies on scm.autoReveal being on (VS Code default) — if you turn scm.autoReveal off, or the primary has no changes, the primary is collapsed along with the rest. Regardless of this setting, you can always fold manually via the 'Collapse all worktree / repository sections in Source Control' command (which also keeps the primary expanded).

better-git-vscode.newFileNavLineJump.description: For a genuinely NEW file (untracked / staged-new / intent-to-add) shown as a plain editor, the next/previous-change shortcuts have no per-change hunks to jump between — so instead they step the cursor this many lines down/up, letting you scroll through and review the entire new file. When you reach the bottom (or top), they fall through to the next (or previous) changed file as usual. Never affects modified, deleted or renamed files, or any side-by-side diff — those always navigate change-to-change.

better-git-vscode.hunkStagingEnabled.description: Step through a TALL hunk (one taller than the visible editor) in stages using the same next/previous-change keys, instead of the rest running off the bottom of the screen. next-change lands at the top of a tall hunk; pressing next again scrolls DOWN by ~a screenful within that same hunk; repeat until the bottom is on screen, then the next press advances to the next hunk. previous-change mirrors it (step UP in stages, then advance to the previous hunk). Hunks that already fit on screen are unaffected — one press goes straight to the next/previous hunk exactly as before. Turn this off to always jump hunk-to-hunk.

better-git-vscode.hunkStagingThreshold.description: Minimum hunk height, in lines, before tall-hunk staging engages. 0 (default) = AUTO: use the real height of your visible editor (from the viewport), so staging kicks in exactly when a hunk can't fit on one screen — and adapts automatically to your window size. Set a positive number to override with a fixed line count (e.g. 40 = only stage hunks taller than 40 lines). Only applies when Tall-hunk staging is enabled.

better-git-vscode.hunkStagingLineStep.description: How many lines each within-hunk step scrolls while stepping through a tall hunk. 0 (default) = AUTO: one viewport height minus the overlap, so each press advances roughly a screenful while keeping a few lines of context. Set a positive number to override with a fixed step (e.g. 30). Only applies when Tall-hunk staging is enabled.

better-git-vscode.hunkStagingOverlap.description: How many lines of overlap to keep on screen between consecutive tall-hunk steps, so reading context carries across each step (a blind full-page jump loses your place). Default 4. Used by the AUTO step size (viewport minus this overlap) and to decide when the remaining tail of a hunk is too small to bother stepping (it just advances to the next hunk instead). Only applies when Tall-hunk staging is enabled.

better-git-vscode.debugLogging.description: Log tall-hunk stepping decisions to the 'Better Git' output channel (View → Output → pick 'Better Git' from the dropdown). Each next/previous-change press logs the direction, viewport height, hunk extent, top/bottom visible lines, caret line, computed target, remaining lines, and the decision taken (stepped / advanced / reached-end). Off by default (no noise); turn on only when diagnosing a stepping issue.

_comment_dvorakMode_keybindings: LAYOUT KEYBINDINGS — the single-source-of-truth architecture (package.json is strict JSON,
so this human-readable explanation lives in this ignored `_comment_*` key; VS Code ignores
any contributes key it doesn't recognise).
THE CANONICAL BEHAVIOUR lives in exactly TWO commands: `next-scm-change` and `previous-scm-change`
(goToNextDiff / goToPreviousDiff in src/extension.ts — hunk-to-hunk nav, new-file scroll mode,
cross-file fallthrough, all the accumulated fixes). Keyboard layouts do NOT get their own behaviour;
they only change WHICH CHARACTER the same physical key produces. Dvorak mode is therefore a THIN
KEY-REMAP WRAPPER: same commands, different character labels for the same physical keys.
The design centre is the PHYSICAL `>` and `<` keys (the . and , keycaps — the arrows literally
point forward/back):
physical > key : QWERTY types `.`  -> alt+.  | Dvorak types `v` -> alt+v  => next-scm-change
physical < key : QWERTY types `,`  -> alt+,  | Dvorak types `w` -> alt+w  => previous-scm-change
shift + the same physical key = stage-and-next / stage-and-previous, in BOTH layouts
(QWERTY shift+alt+. / shift+alt+, ; Dvorak shift+alt+v / shift+alt+w).
The gate is VS Code's native `config.<settingId>` when-clause support on the boolean setting
`better-git-vscode.dvorakMode` — `when: config...dvorakMode` enables the Dvorak-character entry,
`!config...dvorakMode` enables the QWERTY-character entry. Exactly one entry per command is live at
a time; toggling the setting swaps them with no restart. User keybindings.json entries still win.
HISTORY OF THE v1.2.5 FIX (why next/previous-scm-change moved from alt+z/alt+a to alt+./alt+,):
Before v1.2.5 the QWERTY >/< keys (alt+./alt+,) were bound to smart-forward/smart-back instead.
Those are Ethan's MOUSE-button commands (Karabiner F13/F17), and commit 6043d05 intentionally
FLIPPED their in-diff direction for the mouse (forward = PREVIOUS change — his preference for the
thumb buttons). Because the keyboard >/< keys shared those commands, QWERTY keyboards inherited the
mouse flip: pressing > went BACKWARD through changes, < went FORWARD, and outside a diff the keys
did editor history navigation instead of change navigation. Dvorak was immune the whole time —
its physical >/< keys type v/w, which hit next/previous-scm-change directly. That working Dvorak
path is the source of truth; v1.2.5 reverse-maps it back onto QWERTY so both layouts run the
identical canonical commands from the identical physical keys. The old alt+z/alt+a QWERTY defaults
were dropped: alt+z was shadowing VS Code's built-in Toggle Word Wrap (a real conflict — that
default is restored now), and alt+a had no mnemonic; >/< is the advertised, correct home.
smart-forward/smart-back KEEP their alt+./alt+, defaults ONLY under dvorakMode (where those
characters sit on different physical keys and don't collide with v/w) so Ethan's working Dvorak
setup is preserved byte-for-byte. On QWERTY they ship NO default keyboard binding — they are
mouse-button commands (bind F13/F17 or your own key in keybindings.json if you want them). Do NOT
give them QWERTY defaults on >/< again; that is exactly the bug v1.2.5 fixed.
Everything else (changed-file nav cmd+alt+,/cmd+alt+. , alt+q revert, alt+r reveal) is LEFT ON ITS
DEFAULT regardless of dvorakMode — those characters are reachable/comfortable on both layouts and
Ethan never remapped them.
_comment_ctrlShiftG_keybinding: ctrl+shift+g (the LAST entry in `keybindings` below) is a COMPOSITE binding built on VS Code's
built-in `runCommands` command. `runCommands` runs each id in `args.commands` in order, and it IS
supported directly inside contributes.keybindings (the manifest keybindings go through the SAME
resolver as a user keybindings.json, so the args-bearing form works identically here — no extension
code needed). We use it so one chord does two things in sequence:
1. better-git-vscode.open-change-at-position — open the active file's side-by-side diff scrolled to
the EXACT cursor + top line you were viewing in the plain file (our new mirror-of-reveal command).
2. workbench.view.scm — then focus/open the Source Control pane so the file is highlighted there.
WHY ctrl+shift+g: it shadows VS Code's default 'show Source Control' chord, which only did step 2 —
this upgrades it to 'jump into this file's changes at my position AND show Source Control'. We bind the
SAME key on mac + win/linux (ctrl, not cmd) on purpose: cmd+shift+g is taken on mac (find-next-match),
so ctrl+shift+g is the cross-platform-consistent choice. Users can override/disable it in their own
keybindings.json exactly like every other default (a `-runCommands` minus-entry on ctrl+shift+g).

scripts.pretest: npm run compile-tests && npm run compile && npm run lint
